splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing rules for actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original expiration date.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is not tolled during absconding because the statute does not provide for tolling, and courts lack authority to judicially create such a doctrine to extend punishment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1)—which categorically bars claims previously presented—when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are barred from re-raising any previously presented claim; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standards for evaluating whether candidates have pre-election standing to challenge voting procedures. The resolution affects whether challenges to mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and election administration practices can be brought before an election or must wait until after, with significant practical consequences for election litigation nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury from election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes, giving them Article III standing to challenge such rules before an election.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates fail to demonstrate a concrete and particularized injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging election rules that have not yet caused them measurable harm.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference when construing ambiguous provisions that carry criminal consequences. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails, while the Third Circuit has held that deference trumps lenity. The issue has grown more salient after Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo cast doubt on strong deference doctrines.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions even in criminal sentencing contexts, rather than applying the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means a defendant must meet all three listed criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. This directly determines whether defendants facing mandatory minimums can receive lower sentences, creating geographic disparities in criminal sentencing.

positions.label: Conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' is conjunctive, so defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1).

positions.label: Disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' is disjunctive (or distributive), so possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid and authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson and Kisor.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary expanding the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy is entitled to controlling deference. Some circuits defer to Application Note 1, while others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, making the commentary an impermissible expansion rather than an interpretation.

positions.label: Commentary is valid and entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of genuinely ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson, even after Kisor's narrowing of deference.

positions.label: Commentary is invalid — guideline text is unambiguous
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline rather than interpreting it, and is not entitled to deference after Kisor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state's drug schedules are broader than the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) requires that the substance be federally controlled or encompasses any substance controlled under state law. This affects career offender designations and base offense levels for defendants with prior state drug convictions involving substances not on federal schedules.

positions.label: State law controls — no federal limitation
positions.summary: Holds that there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the career offender guideline; any offense under state law punishing distribution of a controlled substance qualifies.

positions.label: Federal schedules control
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to the analysis, while others allow courts to consider them, at least in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to address this, but circuits disagree on its validity.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes can be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have held that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects debtors' post-petition appreciation upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects debtors who convert in good faith from losing post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation to the estate, consistent with the statute's structure and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody must proceed under habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. § 2241) or may bring claims through other civil actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether challenges to BOP decisions—such as denials of sentence credits or transfers to prerelease custody under the First Step Act—that affect the duration of confinement must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus or may also be pursued through civil rights actions. This affects the procedural avenues available to federal prisoners seeking to vindicate statutory rights.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges the fact or duration of confinement, habeas corpus under § 2241 is the exclusive federal remedy, precluding civil rights or APA actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions or BOP decisions may proceed as civil rights actions even when they arguably affect the duration of custody, especially where habeas relief would be inadequate.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. United States Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment order (attorney's fees in criminal cases) is governed by the civil filing deadline (60 days under FRAP 4(a)) or the criminal deadline (14 days under FRAP 4(b)).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil or criminal matter for purposes of determining the appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but acknowledged a lopsided circuit split on the question.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (60 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 60-day civil appeal deadline under FRAP 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee determination arises from a criminal case and therefore the shorter criminal appeal deadline under FRAP 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying a supervised release violation when imposing a revocation sentence, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of the court's trust.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the permissible purposes of a revocation sentence for supervised release violations. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to punish defendants for the underlying violative conduct, while other circuits limit the revocation sentence to addressing the breach of the court's trust rather than the new criminal conduct itself.

positions.label: Punishment for underlying conduct is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the violations of supervised release conditions, including the underlying criminal conduct, when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust inherent in violating supervised release conditions, not as punishment for the new criminal conduct itself.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the denial of a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine of 28 U.S.C. § 1291.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical information qualify for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order.' Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of orders as immediately appealable, with some requiring that such claims await final judgment.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost if review were delayed.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because the interest in sealing can be adequately vindicated on appeal from final judgment or through other procedural mechanisms.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, including the scope of liability and available remedies?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards governing discrimination claims in business establishments, indicating the circuit's approach may conflict with other circuits. The outcome could impact hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against hospitality and business establishments should be evaluated under broader standards that provide greater protections and remedies to customers.

positions.label: Narrower liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against business establishments should be evaluated under narrower standards that impose more limited obligations on business operators.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to' a broker's services, extends to common-law negligence claims arising from accidents during transportation arranged by the broker. This split has major implications for tort recovery in the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services of arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not regulate the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state tort claims that effectively require pesticide manufacturers to add warnings beyond what the EPA-approved label includes. This split affects product liability litigation involving widely used agricultural chemicals including Roundup.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because such claims impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they run parallel to federal requirements and do not impose additional labeling obligations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims resembling habeas challenges — such as alleged trial errors or sentencing miscalculations — may serve as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors or changes in law affecting a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot be used as a vehicle to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, which must be addressed through habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
